Title: Role of Fine Needle Aspiration Cytology in Thyroid Cancer Diagnosis
Summary: In diagnosing thyroid nodules, FNAC has high sensitivity and specificity for papillary carcinomas but lower reliability for follicular carcinomas where distinguishing them from colloid nodules requires immunohistochemical staining. For other sites like breast and lung, FNAC remains reliable though less conclusive compared to core needle biopsy or surgical pathology.
Based on the newly gathered evidence about FNAC's role in diagnosing various forms of thyroid cancer, particularly highlighting its reduced utility in definitively ruling out follicular carcinomas even when suspicious cells are found under microscopic examination alone, I agree with the original conclusion suggesting option B - follicular carcinoma of thyroid - represents an instance where FNAC might be insufficient. Although FNAC provides valuable preliminary diagnostic clues, confirming follicular carcinoma necessitates additional steps involving specialized stains or possibly repeat sampling techniques to ensure accurate classification since morphologically similar features could mimic benign lesions making precise differentiation crucial yet challenging purely relying only on cellular characteristics observed microscopically during routine cytopathologic assessments conducted via standard FNAC methodologies currently employed clinically across settings ranging outpatient clinics hospital wards emergency departments etc., thus emphasizing the need for caution interpreting negative FNAC reports for follicular variants warranting close monitoring follow-up investigations if suspicion persists Summary: In diagnosing thyroid nodules, FNAC has high sensitivity and specificity for papillary carcinomas but lower reliability for follicular carcinomas where distinguishing them from colloid nodules requires immunohistochemical staining. For other sites like breast and lung, FNAC remains reliable though less conclusive compared to core needle biopsy or surgical pathology.
